title: The Illusion of Authenticity
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
text: The advent of generative AI and 'deepfake' technology has ushered in an era where the boundary between reality and fabrication is increasingly porous. While these tools offer creative possibilities in cinema and art, they pose a significant threat to the integrity of information. The human brain is evolutionarily wired to trust visual evidence; 'seeing is believing' has been a fundamental cognitive shortcut for millennia. However, this shortcut is now a liability. Critical thinking in the digital age requires a shift from passive consumption to active verification. We must now treat every piece of sensational media as a hypothesis rather than a fact. This involves looking for 'artifacts'—subtle glitches in lighting, mismatched shadows, or unnatural blinking patterns—that reveal a video's artificial origin. Beyond technical analysis, one must also evaluate the 'source credibility' and the 'context of dissemination.' Who shared this? What is their motive? Does the content align with established facts from reputable outlets? The danger is not just that we might believe a lie, but that the sheer volume of misinformation will lead to 'reality apathy,' where citizens give up on trying to discern the truth altogether. In this environment, skepticism is no longer a choice; it is a survival skill.

questions.question: What is the writer's view on the human tendency to trust visual evidence?

options.A: It remains our most reliable way to understand the world.
options.B: It has become a weakness due to new technological developments.
options.C: It is a skill that children must be taught in school.
options.D: It is only useful in the context of cinema and the arts.

questions.question: How does the writer suggest we should view sensational media?

options.A: As a reliable source of entertainment.
options.B: As a statement that requires evidence to be proven true.
options.C: As something that should be ignored entirely.
options.D: As an accurate reflection of public opinion.

options.A: historical documents found on the internet.
options.B: small errors in a video that suggest it was faked.
options.C: the physical components of a computer system.
options.D: the positive creative outputs of generative AI.

questions.question: What does the process of evaluating 'source credibility' involve?

options.A: Checking how many people have liked or shared a post.
options.B: Investigating the identity and intentions of the person sharing the media.
options.C: Focusing only on the technical quality of the video.
options.D: Asking friends and family for their personal opinions.

options.A: A lack of interest in current political events.
options.B: A state where people stop caring about what is true and what is false.
options.C: A medical condition caused by excessive screen time.
options.D: The belief that technology can solve all human problems.

questions.question: In the final sentence, the writer implies that skepticism...

options.A: is a negative trait that ruins social interaction.
options.B: is essential for navigating the modern information landscape.
options.C: is something only experts and scientists should practice.
options.D: will eventually be replaced by better AI detection tools.
